name: Famous Inventions & Inventors
description: From the wheel to the World Wide Web — the breakthroughs that shaped civilization. 5 eras of human ingenuity, patent wars, accidental discoveries, and the inventors behind them.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the oldest known wooden wheel with an axle?
facts.explanation: The Ljubljana Marshes Wheel (Slovenia), dated to approximately 3130 BCE, is the oldest known wooden wheel with an axle.

variants.question: In which country was the oldest known wooden wheel with axle found?

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately when was the Ljubljana Marshes Wheel (oldest known wooden wheel with axle) dated to?
facts.explanation: The Ljubljana Marshes Wheel is dated to approximately 3130 BCE, placing it in the Copper Age.

variants.question: The Ljubljana Marshes Wheel dates to which prehistoric age?

facts.quizQuestion: Clay tablet pictographs depicting wheeled wagons from Uruk, Sumer date to approximately what period?
facts.explanation: Clay tablet pictographs from Uruk, Sumer depict wheeled wagons dated to c. 3500–3350 BCE.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Han dynasty official is traditionally credited with inventing paper around 105 CE?
facts.explanation: Cai Lun, an official attached to the Imperial court, is traditionally credited with refining papermaking around 105 CE using mulberry fibers, fishnets, rags, and hemp waste.

variants.question: In what dynasty did Cai Lun refine the papermaking process?

facts.correctAnswer: Mulberry fibers, fishnets, old rags, and hemp waste
facts.quizQuestion: What materials did Cai Lun use to improve papermaking around 105 CE?
facts.explanation: Cai Lun used mulberry and other bast fibres along with fishnets, old rags, and hemp waste to create his improved paper.

variants.question: How widespread had paper become by the 3rd century CE in China?

facts.quizQuestion: Around what year did Johannes Gutenberg invent the movable-type printing press?
facts.explanation: Johannes Gutenberg invented the movable-type printing press around 1440 in Germany, with a 1439 lawsuit mentioning 'Gutenberg's types' as the earliest official record.

facts.quizQuestion: How many pages per workday could a Renaissance printing press produce?
facts.explanation: A Renaissance press could produce up to 3,600 pages per workday, compared to approximately 40 pages by hand-copying.

variants.question: By 1500, how many copies had printing presses already produced in Europe?

facts.quizQuestion: What metal alloy did Gutenberg use to make his movable type blocks?
facts.explanation: Gutenberg's key innovation was metal type made from an alloy of lead, tin, and antimony, enabling rapid production of uniform letter blocks.

variants.question: How many cities in Europe had printing presses by end of the 15th century?

facts.quizQuestion: During which Chinese dynasty was the compass invented using lodestone?
facts.explanation: The compass was invented in China during the Han dynasty (c. 206 BC onwards) using lodestone, initially as a 'south-pointing spoon' for geomancers.

variants.question: What was the original use of the 'south-pointing spoon' compass in China?

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately when did the compass reach Western Europe and the Islamic world?
facts.explanation: The compass reached Western Europe and the Islamic world around 1190, having been invented in China during the Han dynasty.

variants.question: The compass is one of China's how many 'Great Inventions'?

facts.quizQuestion: During which Chinese dynasty was gunpowder invented?
facts.explanation: Gunpowder was invented in China during the late Tang dynasty in the 9th century CE, discovered by Taoist alchemists seeking longevity elixirs.

variants.question: Who discovered gunpowder in China — what group of people?

facts.quizQuestion: What year does the first confirmed written formula for gunpowder appear?
facts.explanation: The first confirmed gunpowder formula appears in a Taoist text from 808 CE: 'six parts sulfur to six parts saltpeter to one part birthwort herb.'

variants.question: What was the first military use of gunpowder in China?

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of the first Roman aqueduct, built in 312 BC?
facts.explanation: The Aqua Appia, built in 312 BC and commissioned by censor Appius Claudius Caecus, was the first Roman aqueduct.

variants.question: How many aqueducts did Rome have by the late 3rd century AD?

facts.quizQuestion: How did Roman aqueducts move water — what force drove the flow?
facts.explanation: Roman aqueducts moved water by gravity alone along a slight downward gradient — no pumps were used. Vitruvius recommended gradients of no less than 1 in 4800.

variants.question: What percentage of Roman aqueduct conduit length was carried above ground?

facts.quizQuestion: What is the most famous example of Roman concrete construction still standing?
facts.explanation: The Pantheon dome in Rome is the most famous example of Roman concrete — described as 'the world's largest and oldest unreinforced concrete dome.'

variants.question: What ingredient gave Roman marine concrete its exceptional durability?
variants.correct_answer: Pozzolana (volcanic ash from Bay of Naples)

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented glassblowing in the mid-1st century BC?
facts.explanation: Glassblowing was invented in the mid-1st century BC by Phoenician and Syrian craftsmen in the Syro-Palestinian region (contemporary Lebanon/Israel), enabling mass production of diverse glass objects.

facts.quizQuestion: Who introduced the Bessemer process for mass-producing cheap steel in 1855?
facts.explanation: Henry Bessemer introduced the Bessemer process in 1855, making mass production of cheap steel possible and replacing wrought iron for most purposes.

variants.question: Where was the earliest known steel found, dated to around 1800 BC?

facts.quizQuestion: During which Chinese dynasty was the stirrup invented in the early 4th century AD?
facts.explanation: The stirrup was invented in China during the Jin dynasty in the early 4th century AD. The earliest archaeological evidence is a funerary figurine depicting a stirrup dated 302 AD.

variants.question: By what century did European adoption of the stirrup become widespread?

facts.quizQuestion: How many overshot waterwheels did the ancient Barbegal mill complex in France have?
facts.explanation: The Barbegal mill complex in southern France (2nd century AD) had 16 overshot waterwheels and could produce 4.5 tons of flour daily — the most impressive ancient mill installation.

facts.quizQuestion: Where was the first windmill (panemone type) invented around 700–900 AD?
facts.explanation: The first windmill was invented around 700–900 AD in Persia's Khorasan region (eastern Iran and western Afghanistan), with vertical wooden sails on a central vertical shaft.

facts.quizQuestion: What key mechanism made the first mechanical clocks possible around 1300?
facts.explanation: The verge escapement mechanism was the key innovation that made mechanical clocks possible, first appearing around 1300 in Europe.

variants.question: What were the early purposes of mechanical clocks around 1300?
variants.correct_answer: Signaling for ecclesiastical services and modeling astronomical phenomena

facts.quizQuestion: Where did Wootz steel, an early high-quality steel, emerge during the 1st millennium BC?
facts.explanation: Wootz steel emerged in Southern India and Sri Lanka during the 1st millennium BC, with large-scale crucible production occurring by the sixth century BC.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Chinese dynasty was the first government to issue paper-printed money?
facts.explanation: The Song dynasty (960–1279 CE) became the first government to issue paper-printed money, building on the papermaking tradition refined by Cai Lun.

variants.question: Paper had become widespread by what century in China?

facts.quizQuestion: What mineral forms in Roman marine concrete when seawater reacts with volcanic ash, making it self-healing?
facts.explanation: In marine structures, seawater reacts with the volcanic ash (pozzolana) in Roman concrete to create tobermorite crystals — described as 'the most durable building material in human history.'

variants.question: From where did Roman concrete get its key pozzolana ingredient?

facts.quizQuestion: By what year did iron needles magnetized by lodestone become the standard compass design in China?
facts.explanation: By 1088, during the Song dynasty, iron needles magnetized by lodestone became the standard compass design in China.

variants.question: When was the dry compass (pivoting needle without liquid) invented in Medieval Europe?

facts.quizQuestion: What are the earliest known glass objects, dated to the mid-third millennium BC?
facts.explanation: The earliest known glass objects are beads dating to the mid-third millennium BC (~2500 BCE), possibly created accidentally during metalworking.

variants.question: What natural glass did Stone Age societies use for cutting tools?

facts.quizQuestion: Through which Central Asian people did the stirrup first reach Europe?
facts.explanation: Iron stirrups found in 7th century Avar graves in Hungary indicate the Avar nomadic people brought the stirrup from Central Asia to Europe.

variants.question: What was the earliest archaeological evidence for the stirrup, and when was it dated?
variants.correct_answer: A funerary figurine from near Changsha, dated 302 AD

facts.quizQuestion: Which ancient Greek engineer's technical treatises provide the earliest evidence for watermills, circa 280–220 BC?
facts.explanation: The earliest evidence for watermills appears in Greek engineer Philo of Byzantium's technical treatises (Pneumatica, Parasceuastica), circa 280–220 BC.

variants.question: Where was the first horizontal-wheeled watermill invented, in the first half of the 3rd century BC?

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many wind-powered mills existed at their peak usage around 1850?
facts.explanation: At their peak around 1850, an estimated 200,000 wind-powered mills operated globally — about half of all mills at the time.

variants.question: By what century did the vertical windmill appear in Northwestern Europe?

facts.quizQuestion: What was the combined conduit length of Rome's 11 aqueducts by the late 3rd century AD?
facts.explanation: By the late 3rd century AD, Rome's 11 aqueducts had a combined conduit length of 780–800 km, with only about 47 km (6%) carried above ground.

variants.question: How much water per day did the first Roman aqueduct, Aqua Appia, discharge into Rome?

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the first commercially successful engine that could transmit continuous power to a machine, around 1712?
facts.explanation: Thomas Newcomen created the first commercially successful engine that could transmit continuous power to a machine around 1712, primarily used for mine pumping.

variants.question: What was Thomas Newcomen's steam engine primarily used for?

facts.quizQuestion: What key improvement did James Watt add to the steam engine around 1763–1769?
facts.explanation: James Watt's critical improvement was a separate condenser, which meant his engines used half as much coal as improved Newcomen designs.

variants.question: When did Watt's steam engine patent expire, opening the way for high-pressure engines?

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented the Spinning Jenny in 1764–1765?
facts.explanation: James Hargreaves invented the Spinning Jenny in 1764–1765 in Stanhill, Oswaldtwistle, Lancashire, receiving patent no. 962 on July 12, 1770.

variants.question: How many spindles did early Spinning Jennies have, and how many did later versions reach?

facts.quizQuestion: How many spindles did the original Spinning Jenny have?
facts.explanation: The Spinning Jenny was initially designed with 8 spindles, though technology advanced to allow 120 spindles as the machines improved.

variants.question: What bottleneck did the Spinning Jenny address in the textile industry?
variants.correct_answer: Cotton yarn production could not keep up with demand

facts.quizQuestion: Who patented the power loom in 1785?
facts.explanation: Edmund Cartwright patented the power loom in 1785, pioneering automated weaving. By 1787 he developed water-powered versions, later adapted for steam power.

variants.question: What type of power did Cartwright's 1787 power loom use?

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the cotton gin in 1793?
facts.explanation: Eli Whitney created the cotton gin in 1793, applying for a patent on October 28, 1793. It could clean 50 pounds of lint per day.

variants.question: How much cotton lint per day could Eli Whitney's cotton gin clean?

acceptableAlternatives: It intensified slavery — slave population grew from ~700,000 in 1790 to ~3.2 million by 1850
facts.quizQuestion: How did the cotton gin paradoxically affect slavery in the United States?
facts.explanation: Slave population grew from ~700K to ~3.2M. The cotton gin paradoxically intensified slavery, with the slave population growing from ~700,000 in 1790 to ~3.2 million in 1850 as cotton production soared.
distractors: It reduced slavery by making labor less necessary
It had no measurable effect on slavery
It slowly phased out slavery through industrialization
It replaced slaves with machines in cotton fields
It reduced cotton demand and thus slave dependency

variants.question: How many bales of cotton did U.S. production reach by 1850 after the cotton gin?

facts.quizQuestion: Who designed and built the Rocket locomotive that won the 1829 Rainhill Trials?
facts.explanation: Robert Stephenson designed and built the Rocket in 1829 at Forth Street Works, Newcastle (with advice from his father George Stephenson), winning the Rainhill Trials.

variants.question: What was the maximum speed of Stephenson's Rocket locomotive?

facts.quizQuestion: How many copper tubes did the Rocket's innovative multi-tubular boiler contain?
facts.explanation: The Rocket's multi-tubular boiler contained 25 copper tubes, a key technical innovation along with a blastpipe for improved draft. The Rocket became the template for steam engines for the following 150 years.

variants.question: The Rainhill Trials required locomotives to exceed what speed?

facts.quizQuestion: What was the first famous message sent by Morse's telegraph on May 24, 1844?
facts.explanation: The first famous telegraph message, 'WHAT HATH GOD WROUGHT,' was sent on May 24, 1844 from the U.S. Capitol in Washington to Baltimore.

variants.question: Who was Samuel Morse's assistant who helped create the register for the electric telegraph?

facts.quizQuestion: Who patented a competing five-needle telegraph system in Britain in May 1837, predating Morse commercially?
facts.explanation: Cooke and Wheatstone patented a competing five-needle telegraph system in Britain in May 1837, predating Morse's commercial patent.

variants.question: By what year did an international conference adopt a modified Morse code as global standard?

facts.quizQuestion: Who patented the lockstitch sewing machine in 1845 and won a patent infringement case against Singer in 1854?
facts.explanation: Elias Howe patented his lockstitch sewing machine in 1845 and won a patent infringement case against Isaac Singer in 1854, earning $1.15 per machine sold.

variants.question: How much per machine did Isaac Singer pay Elias Howe under license after losing the 1854 patent case?

facts.quizQuestion: What ingredient did Alfred Nobel stabilize to create dynamite in 1866?
facts.explanation: Alfred Nobel stabilized the dangerously unstable nitroglycerin by mixing it with absorbent materials (diatomaceous earth/powdered shells/clay) to create dynamite in 1866.

variants.question: What did Alfred Nobel originally intend to call dynamite?

facts.quizQuestion: From which ancient Greek word meaning 'power' is the name 'dynamite' derived?
facts.explanation: The name dynamite is derived from the Ancient Greek dýnamis meaning 'power.' Nobel also invented the blasting cap for controlled remote detonation.

variants.question: In which country did Alfred Nobel first patent dynamite?

facts.correctAnswer: Mr. Watson, come here, I want to see you
facts.quizQuestion: What were the first words successfully transmitted by telephone, spoken by Alexander Graham Bell on March 10, 1876?
facts.explanation: On March 10, 1876, Bell spoke the first words successfully transmitted by telephone: 'Mr. Watson, come here, I want to see you.'
distractors: Hello, can you hear me clearly?
What hath God wrought this day
I have invented the telephone today
Speak clearly and I shall hear

variants.question: When did Bell receive the first US patent for the electric telephone?

facts.quizQuestion: Who filed a telephone patent caveat on the same day as Bell — February 14, 1876 — but later abandoned it?
facts.explanation: Elisha Gray filed a patent caveat the same morning as Bell on February 14, 1876. Bell's application was processed immediately while Gray's caveat remained in the in-basket. Gray later abandoned his caveat.

variants.question: What patent number did Bell's telephone patent receive?

facts.quizQuestion: What nursery rhyme is associated with Edison's first words recorded on the phonograph?
facts.explanation: Edison recounted reciting 'Mary Had a Little Lamb' in his initial phonograph test. The phonograph was announced publicly on November 21, 1877.

variants.question: When did Edison publicly announce the phonograph?

facts.quizQuestion: What material was the cylinder wrapped with on Edison's original phonograph?
facts.explanation: Edison's phonograph used a tinfoil-wrapped cylinder. Sound vibrations caused a stylus to indent the foil, creating 'hill-and-dale' variations that could be played back.

variants.question: What earlier device recorded sound but could not play it back, which Edison built upon?
variants.correct_answer: The phonautograph (by Édouard-Léon Scott de Martinville, 1857)

facts.quizQuestion: How long did Edison's first successful light bulb test last on October 22, 1879?
facts.explanation: Edison's first successful light bulb test on October 22, 1879 lasted 13.5 hours. His team later discovered a carbonized bamboo filament could last more than 1,200 hours.

variants.question: What filament material did Edison's team discover could last over 1,200 hours?

facts.quizQuestion: Which London theatre was the first to be lit entirely by electricity in 1881, using Swan bulbs?
facts.explanation: The Savoy Theatre in London (1881) was the first theatre lit entirely by electricity, using Swan bulbs. Edison's real breakthrough was developing not just the bulb but an entire integrated electrical system.

variants.question: What year did Joseph Swan patent his improved incandescent light bulb process?

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the first internal combustion engine that compressed fuel before combustion, in 1876?
facts.explanation: Nicolaus Otto and Langen created the first internal combustion engine that compressed the fuel mixture prior to combustion in 1876, introducing the four-stroke cycle.

variants.question: What approximate thermal efficiency do four-stroke Otto engines achieve?

facts.quizQuestion: When was the daguerreotype — the first publicly available photographic process — formally announced to the world?
facts.explanation: The daguerreotype was formally announced August 19, 1839 at a joint meeting of the French Academy of Sciences and Académie des Beaux-Arts in Paris.

variants.question: How many daguerreotypes were produced annually in the United States alone by 1853?

facts.quizQuestion: What toxic element was used to develop daguerreotype images?
facts.explanation: Daguerreotype images were developed with mercury vapor after the silver-coated copper plate was exposed in camera and sensitized with iodine fumes.

variants.question: Who did Daguerre partner with in 1829, before developing the daguerreotype independently?

facts.quizQuestion: Which 1874 typewriter established the QWERTY keyboard layout?
facts.explanation: The QWERTY keyboard layout was established by the 1874 Sholes & Glidden typewriter, though the exact reason for the layout 'still needs to be clarified.'

variants.question: What company began manufacturing Sholes's typewriter on March 1, 1873?

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented the Laufmaschine (draisine) in 1817 — the first commercially successful two-wheeled steerable machine?
facts.explanation: Karl von Drais invented the Laufmaschine (draisine) in 1817. His inaugural ride on June 12, 1817 in Mannheim covered 13 km in under an hour.

variants.question: What year did John Kemp Starley produce the Rover safety bicycle with equal-sized wheels?

facts.quizQuestion: Who reinvented the pneumatic tire for bicycles in 1888?
facts.explanation: John Dunlop reinvented the pneumatic tire in 1888, dramatically improving bicycle comfort and performance.

variants.question: What was the first commercially successful pedal-driven bicycle called, produced around 1863–1868?

facts.quizQuestion: How did Charles Goodyear accidentally discover vulcanization in 1839?
facts.explanation: Goodyear accidentally dropped the rubber and sulfur mixture onto a hot frying pan — the rubber hardened rather than melted, leading to the discovery of vulcanization.
distractors: He left rubber in the sun for a week
He mixed rubber with acid accidentally
He froze rubber in a winter storm
He burned rubber in a furnace experiment
He stretched rubber over a steam pipe

variants.question: Who actually patented vulcanization first, slightly before Goodyear, on November 21, 1843?

facts.quizQuestion: Compared to improved Newcomen engines, how much coal did James Watt's steam engine use?
facts.explanation: Watt's engines used half as much coal as improved Newcomen designs, making them far more economical and enabling wider industrial adoption.

variants.question: Who developed high-pressure steam engines for mobile use after Watt's patent expired in 1800?

facts.quizQuestion: What reduced production time for a Model T when Ford's moving assembly line began operation on October 7, 1913?
facts.explanation: Ford's moving assembly line began October 7, 1913 at the Highland Park Ford Plant. It divided the process into 45 steps, reducing production time from 12.5 man-hours to just 93 minutes.

variants.question: What industry reportedly inspired Ford's concept for the moving assembly line?

facts.quizQuestion: When was Eli Whitney's cotton gin patent finally validated after years of legal delay?
facts.explanation: Whitney applied for his cotton gin patent on October 28, 1793, and it was granted March 14, 1794, but not validated until 1807 due to legal challenges.

variants.question: How many bales of cotton did U.S. production reach in 1830?

facts.quizQuestion: What are the four strokes of the Otto engine cycle in order?
facts.explanation: The four-stroke cycle: Intake (piston draws air-fuel mixture), Compression (piston compresses mixture), Power/Combustion (spark ignites, piston forced down), Exhaust (piston expels burnt gases).

variants.question: What type of engine remains the most common internal combustion design for motorized land transport?

facts.quizQuestion: How much did Ford's Model T cost in 1908, before the assembly line drastically reduced the price?
facts.explanation: The Model T cost approximately $825 in 1908. The moving assembly line allowed Ford to reduce this to approximately $575 by 1912.

variants.question: How fast did Model Ts move off the assembly line — what interval?

facts.quizQuestion: What year did Isaac Singer patent his improved sewing machine?
facts.explanation: Isaac Singer patented his improved sewing machine in 1851, featuring a falling shuttle, vertical needle, and presser foot. He later paid Elias Howe $1.15 per machine under license.

variants.question: What type of stitch does the modern lockstitch sewing machine create?
variants.correct_answer: Two threads (needle thread and bobbin thread) interlaced at each needle hole

facts.quizQuestion: In which city did Christopher Latham Sholes patent the first commercially successful typewriter in 1868?
facts.explanation: Sholes, Frank Haven Hall, Carlos Glidden, and Samuel W. Soule patented the first commercially successful typewriter in 1868 in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.

variants.question: In what city did Remington begin manufacturing the Sholes typewriter on March 1, 1873?

facts.quizQuestion: When did Marconi transmit the first transatlantic radio signal?
facts.explanation: Marconi transmitted the first transatlantic radio signal on December 12, 1901. In 1895 he had already developed the first radio communication system using a spark-gap transmitter.

variants.question: Which Nobel Prize did Marconi and Karl Ferdinand Braun share in 1909?
variants.correct_answer: Nobel Prize in Physics (for wireless telegraphy)

facts.quizQuestion: Who experimentally confirmed Maxwell's electromagnetic wave theory in 1886, paving the way for radio?
facts.explanation: Heinrich Hertz experimentally confirmed Maxwell's electromagnetic wave theory in 1886. This work was later applied by Marconi to develop practical radio communication.

variants.question: What did the U.S. Supreme Court rule in 1943 about Tesla's radio patents vs. Marconi's?
variants.correct_answer: Tesla's radio patents had priority over Marconi's in some areas

facts.quizQuestion: When did Wilhelm Röntgen discover X-rays?
facts.explanation: Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays on November 8, 1895 while experimenting with Lenard tubes and Crookes tubes — noticing a fluorescent screen glowing despite the tube being covered.

variants.question: Which Nobel Prize did Röntgen receive for discovering X-rays?
variants.correct_answer: Inaugural Nobel Prize in Physics (first ever Nobel Prize in Physics)

facts.quizQuestion: What did Röntgen X-ray on December 22, 1895, producing the first medical X-ray image?
facts.explanation: Röntgen photographed his wife's hand on December 22, 1895 ('Hand mit Ringen'). Upon seeing the image, his wife reportedly said 'I have seen my death.'

variants.question: What did Röntgen's wife reportedly say upon seeing the first X-ray image of her hand?

facts.quizQuestion: On what date did the Wright Brothers make their first successful powered flight?
facts.explanation: On December 17, 1903, at Kill Devil Hills, North Carolina, the Wright Brothers made their first successful powered flight with the Wright Flyer.

variants.question: How far did Orville's first flight travel on December 17, 1903?

acceptableAlternatives: Wing-warping (roll), front elevator (pitch), movable rudder (yaw)
facts.quizQuestion: What three-axis control system did the Wright Brothers develop that made fixed-wing powered flight possible?
facts.explanation: The Wright Brothers' key innovation was a three-axis control system: wing-warping for roll, an elevator at the front for pitch, and a movable rudder for yaw — still standard on all airplanes.
distractors: Aileron flaps, rear elevator, fixed rudder
Wing slots, tail elevator, nose rudder
Roll flaps, pitch control, yaw damper
Variable pitch, roll bars, skid steering

variants.question: How far did Wilbur's final flight of December 17, 1903 travel?

facts.correctAnswer: September 3, 1928, at St. Mary's Hospital, London
facts.quizQuestion: When and where did Alexander Fleming discover penicillin?
facts.explanation: Fleming discovered penicillin on September 3, 1928 at St. Mary's Hospital, London — noticing that fungal contamination of a Staphylococcus aureus culture appeared to kill the bacteria.

variants.question: Who made penicillin medically viable in 1940 by demonstrating its bactericidal action in vivo?
variants.correct_answer: Howard Florey and Ernst Chain at Oxford

facts.quizQuestion: Which three scientists shared the 1945 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for penicillin?
facts.explanation: Alexander Fleming, Howard Florey, and Ernst Chain shared the 1945 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for the discovery and development of penicillin.

variants.question: During which conflict did mass production of penicillin first occur?

facts.quizQuestion: Who gave the first public demonstration of true television — a moving face transmitted by radio — to members of the Royal Institution in January 1926?
facts.explanation: John Logie Baird gave the first true public demonstration of a moving face by radio on January 26, 1926 to members of the Royal Institution, using the Nipkow disk technology.

variants.question: Whose all-electronic television approach became the foundation for modern television?

facts.quizQuestion: When did Philo Farnsworth's image dissector camera tube transmit its first electronic television image?
facts.explanation: Farnsworth's image dissector camera tube transmitted its first image (a straight line) on September 7, 1927 in San Francisco. His all-electronic approach became the foundation for modern television.

variants.question: What technology did John Logie Baird use for his early mechanical television?

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of the 1935 UK experiment where Robert Watson-Watt successfully detected a bomber using radio waves?
facts.explanation: The Daventry Experiment on February 26, 1935 led by Robert Watson-Watt successfully detected a bomber, proving radar's military value. Chain Home was developed from 1936.

variants.question: During which battle did Britain's Chain Home radar system prove vital in providing advance warning?

facts.quizQuestion: What radar component did Britain share with the United States in the 1940 Tizard Mission?
facts.explanation: Britain shared the cavity magnetron technology with the US during the 1940 Tizard Mission. This allowed 'relatively small systems with sub-meter resolution.'

variants.question: Who first used radio waves to detect distant metallic objects in 1904?

facts.quizQuestion: On what date was the first nylon (nylon 66) synthesized at DuPont?
facts.explanation: The first nylon (nylon 66) was synthesized on February 28, 1935, by Wallace Hume Carothers' team at DuPont. Development spanned eleven years from research in 1927 to public announcement in 1938.

variants.question: What was the first nylon commercial product, released in 1938?

facts.quizQuestion: How many pairs of nylon stockings were sold in the first year of commercial sales in 1940?
facts.explanation: Nylon stockings first sold commercially in 1940 — 64 million pairs were sold in the first year. Carothers died before nylon's public announcement and never witnessed its success.

variants.question: Who directed DuPont's polymer research group and synthesized the first nylon?

facts.quizQuestion: When did Frank Whittle submit his initial jet engine patent?
facts.explanation: Frank Whittle submitted his initial jet engine patent on January 16, 1930, which was granted in 1932. Hans von Ohain independently developed a similar design starting in 1935.

variants.question: What was the world's first jet-powered aircraft, and when did it fly?

facts.correctAnswer: Lise Meitner and Otto Robert Frisch
facts.quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'nuclear fission' and provided the theoretical explanation in January 1939?
facts.explanation: Lise Meitner (fled to Sweden) and her nephew Otto Frisch provided the theoretical explanation for nuclear fission in January 1939. Frisch coined the term 'nuclear fission' by analogy to biological cell division.
distractors: Werner Heisenberg and Carl Friedrich von Weizsäcker

variants.question: What element did Hahn and Strassmann find as a product when uranium was bombarded with neutrons in 1938?

facts.correctAnswer: Gave the theory but was excluded from the Nobel Prize
acceptableAlternatives: She provided the theoretical explanation but was controversially excluded from the Nobel Prize
facts.quizQuestion: Why was Lise Meitner notable in the history of nuclear fission discoveries?
facts.explanation: Lise Meitner, an Austrian Jewish physicist who fled to Sweden, provided the theoretical explanation for nuclear fission but was controversially not included in the Nobel Prize.
distractors: She was the first to achieve a sustained chain reaction
She built the first nuclear reactor with Fermi
She invented the Geiger counter for detecting radiation
She was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize in Physics
She designed the atomic bomb's detonation mechanism

variants.question: Who provided the chemical proof of nuclear fission on December 19, 1938?

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of Igor Sikorsky's experimental helicopter publicly demonstrated in 1940?
facts.explanation: Igor Sikorsky's experimental VS-300 helicopter was publicly demonstrated in 1940. The R-4, first flown January 14, 1942, became the world's first large-scale mass-produced helicopter.

variants.question: What was the world's first large-scale mass-produced helicopter?

facts.quizQuestion: When was the first helicopter rescue performed?
facts.explanation: The first helicopter rescue was performed in January 1944 during the USS Turner disaster. The first combat helicopter rescue followed in April 1944 in the China-Burma-India theater.

variants.question: How many Sikorsky R-4 helicopters were built between 1942 and 1944?

facts.quizQuestion: How many vacuum tubes did the ENIAC computer contain?
facts.explanation: ENIAC contained 18,000 vacuum tubes and weighed more than 30 short tons, occupying 300 square feet. It was completed in 1945.

variants.question: What was ENIAC originally created to calculate for the U.S. Army?

facts.quizQuestion: How much did the ENIAC computer cost when completed in 1945?
facts.explanation: ENIAC cost $487,000 (approximately $7 million in 2024 dollars). It was 1,000 times faster than electro-mechanical machines and was first put to practical work on December 10, 1945.

variants.question: Who designed ENIAC?
variants.correct_answer: John Mauchly and J. Presper Eckert at the University of Pennsylvania

facts.quizQuestion: At which research institution was the first working transistor created in December 1947?
facts.explanation: The first working transistor was created at Bell Labs between November 17 and December 23, 1947, by John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley.

variants.question: Which Nobel Prize did Bardeen, Brattain, and Shockley receive for inventing the transistor?

facts.quizQuestion: What percentage of all transistors manufactured worldwide does the MOSFET variant account for?
facts.explanation: The MOSFET variant (invented ~1955–1960) accounts for 99.9% of all transistors manufactured worldwide, making it the most produced device in human history.

variants.question: What does a transistor's base terminal control?
variants.correct_answer: A small current at the base controls a much larger current between collector and emitter

facts.correctAnswer: Burrs clinging to his clothes and dog's fur after hunting
acceptableAlternatives: Burrs clinging to his clothes and dog's fur after a hunting trip
facts.quizQuestion: What inspired George de Mestral to invent Velcro in 1941?
facts.explanation: After a hunting trip in the Alps, de Mestral noticed burrs clinging to clothes and his dog's fur. Microscopic examination revealed hundreds of hooks that caught on anything with a loop.
distractors: A sticky spider web he accidentally walked through

facts.quizQuestion: What organization's adoption of Velcro gave it mainstream acceptance, despite common misconception about who invented it?

facts.quizQuestion: What did Percy Spencer notice melting in his pocket when he accidentally discovered the microwave oven in 1945?

facts.quizQuestion: How much did the first commercial Radarange microwave oven weigh?

variants.question: By what year did over 90% of U.S. households own a microwave oven?

facts.quizQuestion: What was ENIAC's inaugural practical task when put to work in December 1945?
facts.explanation: Though created to calculate artillery firing tables, ENIAC's inaugural practical task (December 10, 1945) was studying the feasibility of thermonuclear weapons.

variants.question: How many times faster was ENIAC than previous electro-mechanical computing machines?

facts.quizQuestion: What was Tesla's ambitious wireless power transmission project called, which was derailed by Marconi's success?

facts.quizQuestion: Into how many steps did Ford's moving assembly line divide Model T production?

facts.quizQuestion: Where did the Wright Brothers make their first powered flight on December 17, 1903?

facts.quizQuestion: Who first demonstrated a working integrated circuit on September 12, 1958?

facts.quizQuestion: What microwave-based predecessor to the laser did Townes and colleagues build in 1953?

facts.quizQuestion: Who disabled GPS's Selective Availability on May 1, 2000, giving civilians full accuracy?
